Rimfire Pacific Mining Says Drilling at New South Wales Target Shows Potential for High-Grade Scandium Mineralization; Shares Jump 9%
data.description:
Rimfire Pacific Mining said that its maiden diamond drilling program at the Murga exploration target in New South Wales demonstrated the potential for high-grade scandium mineralization, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

The company reported significant intercepts from the campaign, including 26.3 meters at 248 parts-per-million (ppm) scandium, or 380 ppm scandium oxide from the surface, as well as 5.9 meters at 364 ppm scandium, or 588 ppm scandium oxide, from the surface.

It also intercepted "unusually anomalous" scandium intersected in underlying pyroxenite rocks. It will use spectral scanning, check assaying, and x-ray crystallography studies to determine the significance of the discovery.

The firm's shares jumped almost 9% in recent trading on Friday.

data.title: Singapore Loans to Residents Rise to SG$841.1 Billion in February
data.description:
Total Loans and advances to residents in Singapore totaled SG$841.1 billion in February, with building and construction loans contributing SG$177.8 billion, according to data released by the Monetary Authority of Singapore on Friday.

The figures indicate an increase in total loans from January's SG$836.3 billion, the data showed.

Loans to businesses stood at SG$515.9 billion, while consumer loans rose to SG$325.2 billion. Housing and bridging loans represented SG$233.0 billion of the total.

News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Jefferies Results Suggest Wall Street Down but Hardly Out

A surprise revenue drop signals caution on dealmaking under Trump.

Fed's Musalem worries that the inflation caused by Trump's tariffs won't be so 'transitory'

Interest-rate hikes may become necessary if upward price movement persists, the St. Louis Fed president says.

What We Lose When the World Goes All-In on Index Investing

Carol Geremia, president of MFS, frets that investors care more about beating indexes and benchmarks and less about finding great long-term investments in companies.

AlbaCore Capital Raises Nearly $2 Billion for Direct Lending Fund

The firm will use the capital to back sectors such as business services and software across Europe.

Bank of Mexico Makes Second Straight Half-Point Rate Cut

The Bank of Mexico lowered its benchmark interest rate by half of a percentage point for the second meeting in a row as inflation eased and the economy continued to slow, and said further cuts of that size are possible.

Churchill Asset Management Closes $750 Million Collateralized Fund Obligation

The firm structured the CFO with a long-duration bond to be secured partly by private-markets assets.

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insights on U.S. homebuyer's monthly payments, UniCredit, Banco BPM, and more in the latest Market Talks covering the Financial Services sector.

Wall Street Bonuses Powered New York's Economy in 2024. Some Worry They Peaked.

The average annual payout jumped 31% to $244,700.

Robinhood Customers Are Growing Up, and the Brokerage Wants to Keep Them

The brokerage that rode the meme-stock wave is rolling out financial services for a more mature set, from investment advice to higher-yielding savings accounts.

Canadian and Mexican Stocks Are Beating the S&P 500. Why Tariffs Aren't Sinking Them.

The U.S. neighbors' main stock indices are outperforming the S&P 500 this year thanks to a unique set of factors.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Economic Growth Now Depends on Electricity, Not Oil

Surging demand for electricity presents huge new investment needs as well as regulatory challenges.

The Frontman Selling Trump's Largest Energy Project to Nervous U.S. Allies

Alaska Gov. Mike Dunleavy is on a mission to convince Asian partners a $44 billion natural gas project is worth it.

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Gain as April Contract Expires

U.S. natural gas futures shook off a bearish storage report and settled higher as the market looked to demand for gas to refill inventories over the summer. The Nymex April contract went off the board at $3.95/mmBtu, up 2.3%, and gas for May delivery settled up 1.4% at $3.925.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Gain insight on fiber operations, oil futures, Repsol's joint venture with NEO Energy, and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

Repsol to Combine U.K. North Sea Upstream Business With NEO Energy

The joint venture could deliver synergies exceeding $1 billion.

Endesa to Buy Back $2.15 Billion of Shares by End of 2027

The first tranche is for up to 17.3 million euros of shares, beginning Friday and set to complete by April 14.

U.S. Crude Oil Inventories Post Unexpected Decline

Oil supplies fell by 3.3 million barrels to 433.6 million in the week ended March 21, below analysts expectations that inventories would rise by 1 million barrels.

Phillips 66 Proposes New Board Members Amid Elliott Battle

The activist investor has pushed the oil refiner to sell or spin off its midstream business.

U.S. Faces Uphill Task to Refill Natural Gas Storage for Next Winter

"People are starting to look around and say where's that additional supply going to come from?" said Eli Rubin, senior energy analyst at EBW Analytics. "Production may be up a bit, but it's not going to keep pace with the demand."

Shell to Increase Production, Shareholder Returns Under New Strategy

The energy giant said it is targeting hydrocarbon production growth and plans to increase shareholder distributions to 40% to 50% of cash flow from operations.

data.title: Iron Ore Prices Lower, Weighed by Subdued Demand — Market Talk
data.description: Iron ore prices are lower in early Asian trade, weighed by subdued demand. Analysts are cautious that the current demand may not sustain given regulatory policies, Baocheng Futures analysts write in a note. However, iron ore's strong fundamentals could support prices ahead, they say. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.6% lower at CNY782.0 a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0236 GMT - Palm oil prices rise in Asian trading, tracking gains in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade as well as palm olein's strength on the Dalian Commodity Exchange, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Palm oil has been fluctuating in sideways trade, awaiting fresh leads, and clearer direction for the market may emerge after the Eid al-Fitr holiday, it adds. AmInvestment Bank sees support for CPO prices at 4,272 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,470 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for June delivery is 74 ringgit higher at 4,386 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0211 GMT - Copper prices fall in early Asian trade amid profit-taking. Expectations that the Trump administration will announce a copper-specific levy in coming weeks has fueled a recent rise in prices, ANZ Research analysts say in a research note. Traders are taking profits as the tariff-driven rally runs out of steam, they say. ANZ thinks any pullback in copper prices is likely to be relatively short-lived, with strong fundamentals providing a backstop. China's copper imports are strong while inventories are falling, the bank notes. The benchmark three-month LME contract is down 0.2% at $9,830.00/metric ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2353 GMT - Gold is steady in the early Asian session after hitting an all-time high overnight. "A series of economic and geopolitical factors have fueled this surge," XS.com's Antonio Di Giacomo says in an email. "Among them, the new tariff measures announced by U.S. President Trump stand out, as they have created uncertainty in international markets," says the financial markets analyst for Latam. This protectionist stance has boosted trade tensions and spurred investors to seek refuge in safe-haven assets like gold, the analyst adds. Spot gold is little changed at $3,055.52/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1909 GMT - Crude futures settle higher with the market weighing the impact of potential supply disruptions against the economic impact of U.S. tariffs. The gradual rally from the March 5 low of $65.22 for WTI could be a mixture of genuine supply concerns around Canadian, Mexican and Venezuelan oil and a technical bounce from a oversold market, Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. "But the rally may be flying a little too close to tariff demand destruction and the OPEC+ production increases that are set to start in April," he adds. WTI settles up 0.4% at $69.92 a barrel, and Brent rises 0.3% to $74.03 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1906 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures shake off a bearish storage report and settle higher as the market looks to demand for gas to refill inventories over the summer. Prices fell after the EIA reported a bigger-than-expected 37 Bcf injection into storage last week, and recovered later in the session. The storage injection reduced the deficit against the five-year average and the year-ago level. The Nymex April contract goes off the board at $3.95/mmBtu, up 2.3%, and gas for May delivery settles up 1.4% at $3.925. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1904 GMT - Weak weekly export sales reported by the USDA weighed on CBOT wheat futures throughout the trading day — with the most-active contract down 0.8% late in the session. The USDA reported wheat sales for the week ended March 20 at 111,500 metric tons in the 2024/25 and 2025/26 marketing years — with Japan and Nigeria being the leading buyers for the week. The total hit the low-end of estimates from analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal this week — and was a factor sending futures down today, says Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting. Setzer adds that soybeans managed to escape the downward pull thanks to speculation about Trump's plans for the Renewable Fuel Standard. Most-active soybeans are up 1.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

The EIA reported a 37 Bcf injection for last week, leaving inventories at 1,744 Bcf and narrowing the deficit against the five-year average and year-ago level. "U.S. Natural Gas Futures Reverse Course — Market Talk," published at 12:37 ET p.m. incorrectly said the EIA reported a 26 BcF injection for last week.

1708 GMT - Base metal prices fall in evening trade, with LME three-month copper down 0.8% to $9,846.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 1.9% to $2,556.50 a ton. The gap between copper contracts on the LME and Comex has widened to record levels in recent days, with traders taking advantage of the arbitrage to shift the metal from global LME warehouses to the U.S. "We think the threat of a 25% tariff as soon as late April/May will heavily discourage physical [arbitrage] trades and associated copper shipments to the U.S. beyond those already en-route," analysts at Citi say. "An associated reduction in U.S. import demand should help ease tightness in the ex-U.S. physical market." The bank still expects near-term downside for LME prices and cuts its forecast to $9,500 a ton from $10,000 previously. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1706 GMT - U.S. tariffs will likely strengthen the dollar in short term, Bank of America economists write. They say the impact so far has been mixed, as concerns about U.S. economic growth offset lower import demand and higher inflation. However, "across-the-board reciprocal tariffs and retaliation could trigger a global risk-off," supporting the greenback. BofA says that hedge funds "have started buying USD again," ahead of the April 2 deadline for new tariffs. The economists say those funds are selling euro, the Norwegian krone and emerging market currencies in all regions. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.1%, as the dollar weakens 0.4% versus the euro and strengthens 0.3% against the yen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1643 GMT - Corn on the CBOT is down for a third consecutive day, with some analysts saying pressure stems from a belief Monday's Prospective Planting report will show planted corn acres over 94 million acres for the 2025/26 marketing year. But the weight on corn futures may be soon lifted, says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing in a note. "December corn is below support as traders are now pricing in four or more million more acres to be planted this spring," says Blohm. "Corn is now oversold heading into Monday's report and the bearish news may be fully priced in as well." Corn futures fall 0.3% and wheat is down 0.8%, while soybeans are up 1.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1637 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures pick up after digesting a bigger-than-expected storage build that had pushed prices lower. The EIA reported a 37 Bcf injection for last week, leaving inventories at 1,744 Bcf and narrowing the deficit against the five-year average and year-ago level. "The fact that the market isn't succumbing to more selling pressure this morning is an indication of strong underlying support," Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors says in a note. Despite an early start to the injection season, stocks will remain at a steep deficit to year-ago levels and "concerns about the pace of injections this summer will remain at the forefront until more material production growth is realized," he adds. The Nymex April contract is up 0.8% at $3.892/mmBtu ahead of today's expiration, and gas for May delivery is up 1.2% at $3.914/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This item was corrected at 2:09 p.m. ET to show that the EIA reported a 37 Bcf injection for last week, not a 26 Bcf injection.

1616 GMT - Gold prices extend gains in evening trade, hitting an all-time high of $3071.30 a troy ounce on fears of escalating trade tensions after U.S. President Donald Trump announced 25% tariffs on automotive imports. Futures trade 1.4% higher at $3063.80/oz. "Safe haven gold has just hit a new record high as trade war uncertainty creates volatility in stock markets," says Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at Forex.com. "The trade war has escalated and there is going to be a lot of back and forth from now until April 2." Traders now await more clarity on the scope of reciprocal tariffs next week, as well as PCE inflation data due this Friday. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Zhongsheng Group 2024 Gross Profit Margin 6.3% Vs. 7.7%>0881.HK
Graphex Group Renews Earthasia Worldwide Revolving Loan Facility
data.description:
Graphex Group has renewed its unsecured revolving loan facility extended to Earthasia Worldwide Holdings with an amount of HK$21 million and the same annual interest rate of 12%, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Under the sixth renewal agreement signed on March 27, the facility remains available from April 1, 2025, to Dec. 31, 2027. The borrower may draw and repay funds within a one-year term.

The outstanding loan balance of the borrower currently stands at HK$10.8 million. Graphex expects the facility to generate reasonable interest income.

data.title: Shengyi Electronics Returns to Profit in 2024
data.description:
Shengyi Electronics returned to profit in 2024 after posting a net profit attributable to shareholders of almost 332.0 million yuan or 0.40 yuan per share, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange filing on Friday.

The attributable loss a year ago was almost 25 million yuan or 0.03 yuan per share.

The Chinese circuit board products manufacturer's operating income jumped 43% year on year to 4.69 billion yuan from 3.27 billion yuan, the filing said.

The company's shares rose less than 2% in recent trade.

data.title: XD Inc. Gross Margin Rose Due to Better Game Gross Margin, Offsetting Lower Rev Contribution From TapTap Platform Business>2400.HK
XD Inc. Declares Final Dividend of HK$0.4 >2400.HK
India's Sensex Edges Lower, Dragged by Tech Stocks — Market Talk
data.description:
0408 GMT - Indian shares are lower as concerns surrounding U.S. tariffs persist. Tech stocks lead the declines, with Infosys down 1.6% and HCL Technologies lower by 0.8%. Among individual movers, Force Motors is up 4.5% after winning a contract to supply nearly 3,000 vehicles to Indian defence forces. Investors are focusing on potential developments related to U.S. trade and foreign policies and their implications for Indian businesses. The Sensex edges down 0.1% at 77500.24. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0401 GMT - Taiwan could face smaller tariff hikes due to its investments in the U.S. and key tech exports, says Lynn Song, ING's Greater China chief economist, in a note. Taiwan has been under scrutiny due to its rising trade surplus with the U.S. over the past years, which amounted to $64.9 billion in 2024. However, Taiwan's key exports of semiconductors and computer products to the U.S. have limited replacement alternatives, raising the probability of being exempted, the economist says. Also, TSMC's expanding investment in the U.S. "may have also brought Taiwan enough goodwill to soften or avert tariffs," he says. As a result, Taiwan could be one of the more likely economies to benefit from a tariff reprieve, or at least smaller tariff hikes, he adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0335 GMT - Ahead of "Liberation Day" in the U.S. as dubbed by President Trump, Australians are looking out to the horizon with trepidation, knowing that a trade war would badly expose the fact that the country relies on the U.S. for its security, while looking to Beijing for its prosperity. Trump is set to announce so-called reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday. With tariffs on cars already announced, other sectoral tariffs will likely be announced in time, says Khoon Goh, head of Asia research at ANZ. While there is scope for negotiations over tariffs, trade uncertainty will remain high and Asia's exports will likely slow, he says. China's attempt to boost consumption will help offset weakness in its exports but may not be sufficient to support the wider region, Goh adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0331 GMT - The Malaysian ringgit is likely to remain range-bound next week, trading between 4.42 and 4.45 against the U.S. dollar, despite ongoing uncertainties surrounding Trump's tariff strategy, Kenanga IB economists say in a note. While the risk-off sentiment may weigh on the ringgit, Malaysia appears relatively insulated and better positioned to withstand U.S. pressures compared with its regional peers, they reckon. Technically, the pair's outlook appears neutral and could hover around 4.432, they add. Kenanga pegs USD/MYR's short-term resistance at 4.436 and support at 4.428. USD/MYR is flat at 4.4320.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0308 GMT - South Korea's export growth was likely sustained in March, after a rebound in February. Exports are tipped to have grown 1.7% from a year earlier, following a revised 0.7% gain in February, according to the median forecast of seven economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. Strong-than-expected shipments of semiconductors and ships may have helped maintain growth, but no solid rebound is likely given the weak recovery in exports to China as well as fresh U.S. tariffs that are expected to take effect in April, iM Securities chief economist Park Sang-hyun says. Imports likely gained 0.7% on year, to result in a $6.00 billion trade surplus in March, following a revised $4.15 billion surplus in the prior month, the poll shows. Trade data are due Tuesday. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0246 GMT - Australia's federal election on May 3 will be a watershed moment for the future of the country's energy supply. Anxiety over soaring electricity costs and business failures has been a major issue in Canberra for some time. The election will determine whether the country's future lies with renewable energy sources under Labor or with a diverse mix of power sources, including nuclear, under the coalition. The coalition also plans to redirect gas typically earmarked for export back into the domestic market. It's a hot-button issue for companies that require energy certainty and something that must be resolved quickly for a country that is otherwise a major supplier of cheap, reliable energy to other parts of the world. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0244 GMT - The economy will likely be a decisive issue in Australia's election campaign, with the date of the poll now set for May 3. For the Labor Party, the economic story is pretty good given that interest rates have been cut, inflation looks tamed, economic growth is gathering momentum and the unemployment rate is at levels not seen for decades. Still, the country has come through a cost-of-living surge, the likes of which hasn't been seen since the 1980s, and the pressure on household budgets remains. The Reserve Bank of Australia has only cut its policy rate once, lagging well behind its global counterparts. Anger over the inflation surge that swamped the economy in the wake of the pandemic may yet see a lot of votes go to the opposition Liberal-National coalition under Peter Dutton. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0229 GMT - Australia's May 3 election will be framed against the backdrop of an increasingly uncertain global economic outlook. The prospect of an escalating global trade war, coupled with a deteriorating geopolitical environment and falling share markets, could have big implications for the country's commodity exports and the outlook for the Australian dollar. The focus of campaigning will be on which candidate will have more sway with Washington. Both the Reserve Bank of Australia and the Department of Treasury are betting that in the midst of the uncertainty, a lower Australian dollar will act as a shock absorber for the economy. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0222 GMT - IHH Healthcare's Turkish unit is likely to maintain its growth momentum despite political uncertainty there, Maybank Investment Bank analysts Nur Natasha Ariza and Yin Shao Yang say in a note. While accounting adjustments related to hyperinflation may weigh on reported earnings amid the Turkish lira's depreciation, core earnings should remain resilient, they say. The Acibadem unit benefits from foreign-currency-denominated medical tourism and steady private healthcare spending, they say. Contributing 30% of IHH's 2024 revenue, the Turkey business is projected to see 9% Ebitda growth in 2025, helped partly by stable in-patient volumes, they add. The analysts remain positive on Acibadem's non-lira income and IHH's other key segments. Maybank maintains a buy rating on IHH and keeps its target price at MYR7.97. Shares are unchanged at MYR6.91. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0214 GMT - ​The calling of a May 3 election in Australia by Prime Minister Anthony Albanese likely makes an interest rate cut by the Reserve Bank of Australia next week less likely. To be sure, most economists were expecting the central bank to pass up the opportunity of an easing given its recent cautious rhetoric about inflation risks. The RBA now has the option of avoiding getting tangled up in the election campaign, and looks likely to take it, deferring any further cut until its next policy meeting in late May. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0155 GMT - The yen strengthens against other G-10 and Asian currencies amid ongoing concerns over U.S. tariffs. In a social-media post overnight, President Trump warned that if the EU and Canada worked together to harm the U.S. economically, they would face levies that were "far larger than currently planned." Trump is expected to unveil reciprocal tariffs on April 2. The upcoming U.S. tariff announcements will likely intensify uncertainty over the global economic outlook, CBA's Kristina Clifton says in a research report. This is a negative for risk-sensitive AUD/JPY, the senior economist and currency strategist adds. AUD/JPY is down 0.4% to 94.88; USD/JPY is 0.2% lower at 150.77. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0135 GMT - Hotter-than-expected consumer-price inflation in Tokyo and signs of broadening pressures support ING's long-held view that the Bank of Japan will raise rates at its two-day meeting ending May 1, says ING's Min Joo Kang. Data released earlier today showed Tokyo CPI inflation unexpectedly accelerated to 2.9% on-year in March, higher than market consensus of 2.7%, the senior economist for South Korea and Japan says in a research report. "We find that price pressures are becoming more broadly based," the economist says. "With upside inflation risks growing, the odds of a BOJ's rate reaction increase," the economist adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

Liaoning Port's Profit Drops 15%, Revenue Slips 9% in 2024; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Liaoning Port's net profit attributable to shareholders in 2024 dropped 15% to 1.14 billion yuan or 0.05 yuan per share from 1.34 billion yuan or 0.06 yuan per share in the preceding year, according to a Hong Kong Stock Exchange filing on Thursday.

The Chinese port operator's revenue fell 9.4% year on year to 11.1 billion yuan from 12.2 billion yuan, the filing said.

The board of directors proposed a final dividend of 0.0239 yuan per share that will be distributed on before Sept. 1.

The company's shares dropped more than 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Citic Declares Final Dividend of CNY0.36 >0267.HK
CSPC Pharmaceutical Chairman: Will Continue Focusing on In-House Pipeline Development With Existing R&D Platforms >1093.HK
Australian Dollar May Trade on Back Foot — Market Talk
data.description: The Reserve Bank of Australia's statement at its coming April 1 meeting is unlikely to be a market mover for the Aussie dollar, TD Securities says, as it expects no change in tone. The RBA is unlikely to shift its 'hawkish' stance and isn't likely to signal an intention to cut as early as May, strategists Prashant Newnaha and Alex Loo say in a note. They see April 2 as the more market-moving event. Trump's reciprocal tariff plans are expected to weigh heavily on the Asian region as 25% of the U.S. trade deficit is with Asia. Australia's strong trade ties to Asia leave it vulnerable, they say, and anticipate the AUD to trade on the back foot versus the USD, revisiting the range lows at 0.62 by end-2Q. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

data.title: China issues 12.8 million tons refined fuel export quotas in 2nd batch of 2025, JLC and traders say
data.description:
China has issued 12.8 million metric tons of refined fuel export quotas in its second batch of allowances this year, less than a year earlier, said China-based consultancy JLC and two trade sources on Friday.

For low-sulphur fuel oil (LSFO), the second batch of export quotas amounted to 5.2 million tons, up 1.2 million tons from last year, they said.

This brings the total volumes for the second batch of export allowances to 18 million tons.

China manages its refined oil exports via a strict quota system, using exports as a tool to balance and ensure the domestic market is sufficiently supplied.

China's commerce ministry did not immediately respond to a faxed query for comment from Reuters.

The second batch of allowances last year was 14 million tons for gasoline, diesel and jet fuel, and 4 million tons for low-sulphur marine fuel.

For this batch of LSFO quota, state-owned oil majors Sinopec and CNPC were granted 4.51 million tons, or 87% of the total volumes, according to JLC.

Exports of refined fuel products for the first two months this year totalled around 4.08 million metric tons, official customs data showed.

Meanwhile, industry estimates for March and April refined fuel exports were at around 3.3 million tons and 3.1 million tons, respectively.

data.title: SICC 2024 Profit Falls 64%; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
SICC posted 2024 net profit attributable to shareholders of 1.01 billion yuan, down 64% from 2.76 billion yuan the previous year.

Earnings per share fell 65% year over year to 0.43 yuan from 1.24 yuan, according to a filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The advanced technologies services provider's revenue declined 13% to 18.7 billion yuan from 21.5 billion yuan.

SICC shares were down 3% in recent trade.
